essential_question: Why can't a triangle ever have two right angles — and why can't 5, 7, and 15 ever close into one?
objective: Justify why a triangle can never have two right angles, why the two shorter sides must outsum the longest, and why every equilateral triangle is also equiangular; review the week's Math-U-See practice; and pass the mastery checkpoint on classifying triangles and applying the triangle inequality.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a scalene triangle, an isosceles triangle, and a right triangle from memory, labeling which is which. Then explain out loud, in your own words, why 'right' and 'obtuse' can never describe the same triangle.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 10A & 10B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 10A/10B. We check the tricky ones: telling 'isosceles right' (real) apart from 'right obtuse' and 'equilateral right' (both impossible), and the triangle-inequality checks that look wrong at first glance — like 6, 7, and 2 — but still have to be tested with the actual rule, not just a gut feeling. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: a triangle can never have two right angles. Argue it from the angle sum — what would be left over for the third angle, and why is that impossible?
Claim 2: sides 5, 7, and 15 cannot form a triangle. Argue it from the triangle inequality — what would the two shorter sides need to add up to instead?
Claim 3: every equilateral triangle is also equiangular, and therefore always acute. Argue it — what forces all three angles to be congruent, and what number does that force each one to equal?

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 11 — Polygons (names, diagonals, and interior/exterior angle sums) — where these triangle ideas scale up to shapes with more sides.

success_criteria.text: I can classify a triangle by its sides and by its angles without help
I can apply the triangle inequality to decide whether three given lengths can form a triangle
I can justify why a triangle can never have two right angles and why some length trios can never close into a triangle

questions.prompt: A triangle with sides measuring 7, 7, and 11 is classified by its sides as…
questions.explain: Two of the three sides (the 7's) are congruent and the third is different — that's isosceles, not equilateral.

questions.prompt: A triangle with angles measuring 20°, 65°, and 95° is classified by its angles as…
questions.explain: One angle (95°) is greater than 90°, and a triangle can only ever have one angle that large.

questions.prompt: Can a triangle have sides measuring 5, 7, and 15? Justify your answer using the triangle inequality.
questions.answer: No — 5 + 7 = 12, which is less than 15. The two shorter sides must add up to MORE than the longest side for three lengths to close into a triangle.
questions.explain: The triangle inequality requires the sum of the two shorter sides to exceed the longest side; here 12 < 15, so it fails.

questions.prompt: Two angles of a triangle measure 90° and 90°. What must the third angle measure?
questions.explain: 180° − 90° − 90° = 0°, but every angle in a real triangle must be greater than 0° — exactly why a triangle can never have two right angles.

questions.prompt: On the grid, construct a triangle that is BOTH isosceles AND right. Label the two congruent sides and mark the 90° angle.
questions.answer: A right triangle with its two legs drawn the same length (tick-marked congruent) and a small square at the right-angle vertex — a 45°-45°-90° triangle.
questions.explain: Rubric: a right angle clearly marked (1 pt) + two congruent sides tick-marked, making it isosceles as well as right (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why a triangle can never have two right angles.
questions.answer: A triangle's three angles must add up to exactly 180°. Two right angles already use up 90° + 90° = 180° by themselves, leaving 0° for the third angle — and no real angle can measure 0°, so a triangle can never have two right angles (or, by the same reasoning, two angles that are each 90° or more).
questions.explain: Full credit: names the 180° angle-sum constraint and shows that two right angles would leave nothing for the third.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 10 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
